name: Hypothermia
immediateActions: Move the person to a warm, dry shelter immediately
Remove any wet clothing
Cover with blankets, sleeping bags, or dry leaves
If conscious, give warm (not hot) sweet drinks
Never rub extremities — this moves cold blood to the core
Apply warm compresses to neck, armpits, and groin only

treatments.description: Gently move victim out of cold and wind. Cut off wet clothes rather than pulling over head if unconscious.
treatments.notes: Handle with extreme care — rough movement can trigger cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia

treatments.description: Insulate from the ground first. Place on sleeping pad or layers of dry material before covering from above.
Apply chemical heat packs or warm water bottles (wrapped in cloth to prevent burns) to neck, armpits, and groin.
treatments.notes: Do not apply heat packs directly to skin — always use a cloth barrier

treatments.description: If conscious and able to swallow, give warm sweet liquids such as sugar water or warm broth. No alcohol.
treatments.notes: Alcohol dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss — never give alcohol for hypothermia

treatments.description: Monitor breathing and pulse continuously. If breathing stops, begin CPR.
treatments.notes: Even a pulse that seems absent may be very slow and weak — check for at least 60 seconds before beginning CPR

warningSigns: Victim stops shivering (paradoxical undressing may follow — a sign of severe hypothermia)
Loss of consciousness
Cardiac arrhythmia or arrest
Breathing becomes imperceptible
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate immediately for any moderate to severe hypothermia. Even mild hypothermia in the wilderness warrants evacuation if rewarming is not possible in the field.
preventionTips: Dress in moisture-wicking layers — never cotton in cold/wet conditions
The rule: 'Cotton kills' — use wool or synthetic base layers
Eat high-calorie foods to maintain metabolic heat
Stay dry — change out of wet clothes immediately
Watch for early shivering and act before progression
Buddy system — watch each other for signs of confusion

name: Heat Stroke
immediateActions: Move to a cool, shady area immediately
Call for emergency help
Cool the person as rapidly as possible — this is the priority
Fan while misting with cool water
Apply ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin
Do NOT give fluids to an unconscious person

treatments.description: Remove from heat source. Move to shade or improvised shade structure. Lay person down with legs elevated if not unconscious.
Remove excess clothing. Fan the victim aggressively while misting skin with cool (not ice cold) water.
treatments.notes: Cold water immersion is most effective if available — full body if possible

treatments.description: Apply ice packs wrapped in cloth to the neck, armpits, and groin where blood vessels are near the surface.
If conscious and able to drink, give cool water or electrolyte solution. Small sips frequently.

warningSigns: Temperature rises above 104°F (40°C)
Person becomes confused or combative
Seizures begin
Breathing becomes labored
whenToEvacuate: Heat stroke is always a medical emergency requiring immediate evacuation and hospital care.
preventionTips: Acclimatize to heat over 7-14 days before strenuous activity
Drink water before you are thirsty — aim for 0.5L per hour in heat
Avoid activity during hottest hours (10am-4pm)
Wear light-colored, loose-fitting, breathable clothing
Wear a hat with full brim
Eat salty foods to replace electrolytes

name: Heat Exhaustion
immediateActions: Stop activity and rest in a cool, shady place
Loosen or remove excess clothing
Apply cool wet cloths to skin
Sip cool water or sports drink if conscious

treatments.description: Stop all activity. Move to shade or cool environment. Lie down with feet elevated 12 inches.
Remove excess clothing. Apply cool damp cloths to forehead, neck, and wrists.
Rehydrate with water or electrolyte solution. 250ml every 15 minutes if tolerating well.
treatments.notes: Add a pinch of salt to water if electrolyte solution unavailable

warningSigns: Symptoms worsen rather than improve with rest and cooling
Person stops sweating (progressing to heat stroke)
Confusion develops
Vomiting prevents rehydration
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate if no improvement within 30 minutes of treatment, or if progressing toward heat stroke symptoms.
preventionTips: Stay well hydrated before and during activity
Take regular shade breaks every hour
Eat salty snacks to maintain electrolyte balance

name: Dehydration
immediateActions: Stop activity and rest in shade
Drink clean water slowly
Add electrolytes if available

treatments.description: Drink 500ml of water over 30 minutes. Do not chug — rapid intake can cause vomiting.
Add electrolytes to water — sports drink, electrolyte tablets, or a pinch of salt with a pinch of sugar.
Continue drinking 250ml every 15-20 minutes until urine returns to pale yellow.
treatments.notes: Urine color is the best field indicator: clear = over-hydrated, pale yellow = good, dark yellow/brown = dehydrated

warningSigns: No urination for 8+ hours
Sunken eyes
Extreme weakness
Confusion or altered mental status (severe dehydration)
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate if unable to keep fluids down, or if severe symptoms develop. IV fluids may be needed for severe cases.
preventionTips: Drink 0.5L of water per hour during activity in warm weather
Start the day well hydrated
Monitor urine color throughout the day
Increase intake at altitude

name: Venomous Snake Bite
immediateActions: Move away from the snake — do not try to capture or kill it
Keep the victim calm and still — movement spreads venom faster
Keep the bitten limb below heart level
Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing from the affected limb
Begin evacuation immediately — antivenom is the definitive treatment

treatments.description: Immobilize the bitten limb with an improvised splint. Keep below heart level.
treatments.notes: Keep the patient as still as possible

treatments.description: Clean the bite wound gently with soap and water if available. Do NOT cut or suck the wound.
treatments.notes: Cutting and sucking is ineffective and introduces infection

treatments.description: Mark the leading edge of swelling with a pen and time — track spread. This information is vital for medical personnel.
Keep the victim calm, still, and lying down. Reassure them. Begin evacuation.
treatments.notes: Anxiety and movement significantly speed venom spread

warningSigns: Rapid, extensive swelling spreading up the limb
Difficulty breathing or swallowing
Altered consciousness
Signs of anaphylaxis (throat tightening, rash, drop in blood pressure)
whenToEvacuate: Immediately — snake bites from potentially venomous species are always a medical emergency. Antivenom must be administered in a hospital.
preventionTips: Watch where you step and place your hands — never reach into unseen spaces
Wear long pants and boots in snake habitat
Use a walking stick to probe ahead when walking through tall grass
Do not pick up or handle any snake in the wild
Most snake bites occur when people try to handle or kill snakes

name: Severe Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis)
immediateActions: Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available
Call for emergency help
Position patient lying down with legs raised unless breathing is difficult (then sit up)
If no EpiPen: keep airway open, be ready to perform CPR

treatments.description: Administer epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into the outer thigh. Can be through clothing.
treatments.notes: If no improvement after 5-15 minutes and a second EpiPen is available, administer a second dose

treatments.description: Keep airway open. If unconscious, place in recovery position. Tilt head back to open airway.
If breathing stops, begin CPR.

warningSigns: Stridor (high-pitched breathing sound) indicating airway narrowing
Cyanosis (blue lips or fingertips)
Rapid decline in consciousness
whenToEvacuate: All anaphylaxis cases require immediate evacuation — effects of epinephrine are temporary and symptoms can return.
preventionTips: Always carry prescribed EpiPen if known allergy history
Wear a medical alert bracelet
Inform fellow scouts of your allergies before trips
Carry antihistamines as a second-line measure (Benadryl)

name: Bone Fracture
symptoms: inability to bear weight or use limb
immediateActions: Do not attempt to straighten a broken bone
Immobilize the fracture in the position found
Check circulation, sensation, and movement below the injury
Splint the fracture including the joint above and below the break

treatments.description: Check CSM (Circulation, Sensation, Movement) below the injury. Pulse, skin color, ability to feel touch, ability to wiggle fingers/toes.
treatments.notes: Document and recheck every 15-20 minutes — loss of CSM is a surgical emergency

treatments.description: Pad the fracture generously. Use clothing, foam, or soft materials around the injured area.
Splint the fracture including the joint above and below the break. Rigid splint (branch, trekking pole, SAM splint) with soft padding inside.
treatments.notes: Splint should be snug but not tight enough to cut circulation

treatments.description: Elevate the injured limb if possible to reduce swelling. Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth if available.
materials: cold pack or snow in cloth

warningSigns: Loss of pulse below the fracture
Skin goes white, blue, or mottled below the injury
Open fracture — bone visible through skin (requires sterile dressing)
Fracture of femur (thigh) — can bleed 1-2 liters internally; treat for shock
whenToEvacuate: All suspected fractures in the wilderness require evacuation. Open fractures and femur fractures are urgent.
preventionTips: Use trekking poles for stability on uneven terrain
Test footing before full weight commitment
Wear appropriate footwear with ankle support
Travel slower in low light conditions

name: Sprain / Strain
immediateActions: Stop activity
Apply RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation

treatments.description: Rest: Stop all weight-bearing activity on the injured joint.
Ice: Apply cold pack or snow wrapped in cloth to injured area for 20 minutes every hour.
treatments.notes: Never apply ice directly to skin — always use cloth barrier

treatments.description: Compression: Apply figure-8 wrap with elastic bandage from foot up to mid-calf for ankle sprains.
treatments.notes: Check circulation regularly — rewrap if fingers or toes become cold, blue, or numb

treatments.description: Elevation: Keep the injured limb raised above heart level when resting.

warningSigns: Unable to bear any weight after 30 minutes of rest (possible fracture)
Significant deformity (possible fracture)
Severe instability
Numbness or tingling
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate if patient cannot walk with support, or if fracture is suspected. Mild sprains may allow slow hiking with taping support.
preventionTips: Warm up properly before hiking
Wear proper footwear with ankle support
Use trekking poles on uneven terrain
Watch for roots, rocks, and unstable ground

name: Blister
immediateActions: Stop and address the blister before it bursts
Clean the area
Apply blister bandage or moleskin donut

treatments.description: If blister is intact: clean with antiseptic, apply donut-shaped moleskin pad around the blister to relieve pressure. Cover with bandage.
treatments.notes: Try to keep the blister intact — the skin over the blister is the best protection

treatments.description: If the blister must be drained (very large or about to burst): sterilize a needle with flame, puncture at the base in 2-3 spots, gently press out fluid.
treatments.notes: Only drain if continuing the hike is required and the blister is severely impairing movement

treatments.description: After draining, do NOT remove the blister roof. Apply antibiotic ointment and a non-stick dressing. Check daily for infection.

warningSigns: Red streaking around blister (sign of infection)
Pus or cloudy fluid
Increasing pain, warmth, and redness
Fever (systemic infection)
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate for signs of infection that do not improve with cleaning and antibiotic ointment, or if fever develops.
preventionTips: Break in new boots before long hikes
Wear moisture-wicking liner socks under hiking socks
Apply moleskin or tape to known hot spots before they blister
Keep feet dry — change socks if wet
Address hot spots immediately before they become blisters

name: Laceration (Cut)
immediateActions: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth
Elevate the wound above heart level if possible
Do not remove embedded objects

treatments.description: Control bleeding: apply firm direct pressure with cleanest material available (sterile dressing if in kit) for a full 10 minutes without lifting.
treatments.notes: Do not lift the bandage to check — this disrupts clot formation. Add more layers on top if blood soaks through.

treatments.description: Once bleeding is controlled, clean the wound thoroughly with clean water using irrigation (pour or squeeze with force). Rinse away all visible dirt.
treatments.notes: The solution to pollution is dilution — clean water irrigation is the most important step in preventing infection

treatments.description: For gaping wounds: close with butterfly bandages, steri-strips, or improvised closure (safety pins, tape strips). Align wound edges carefully.
treatments.notes: Do not close wounds that are very dirty, puncture wounds, or animal bites — these need to drain

treatments.description: Apply antibiotic ointment and a sterile non-stick dressing. Change daily and check for infection.

warningSigns: Bright red blood spurting in pulses (arterial bleeding — apply tourniquet above wound)
Bleeding that does not slow after 10 minutes of pressure
Red streaking extending from wound (infection)
Wound is gaping and will not close (may need stitches)
Signs of tendon damage (inability to move fingers/toes)
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate for uncontrolled bleeding, deep wounds requiring stitches, wounds with signs of infection, or wounds involving tendons or joints.
preventionTips: Always cut away from your body when using knives
Keep knives sharp — dull knives require more force and slip more easily
Use a cutting board or flat surface when food prep
Wear gloves when handling sharp objects or clearing debris

name: Giardia (Waterborne Illness)
immediateActions: Increase fluid intake to prevent dehydration
Rest
Avoid sharing food or utensils

treatments.description: Aggressive rehydration with clean water and electrolytes. Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) if available.
treatments.notes: Giardia does not fully resolve without medical treatment (antibiotics like metronidazole) but symptoms can be managed in the field

treatments.description: Eat bland, easily digestible foods. Avoid dairy, fatty foods, and raw vegetables.

warningSigns: Inability to keep fluids down
Signs of severe dehydration
Blood in stool
High fever
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate if rehydration cannot be maintained, or for medical treatment with antibiotics.
preventionTips: Always purify backcountry water — boil, filter, or treat chemically
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after using the bathroom
Use a WAG bag system — bury waste at least 200 feet from water sources
Never drink from standing water

name: Head Injury / Concussion
symptoms: clear fluid from nose or ears
immediateActions: Do not move the patient if spinal injury is possible
Keep airway open
Monitor level of consciousness continuously
Do not give aspirin or ibuprofen (can increase bleeding)

treatments.description: Stabilize the head and neck if mechanism of injury suggests possible spinal injury (fall, impact). Manual cervical stabilization: hold head in neutral position.
treatments.notes: If patient is breathing and unconscious without spinal injury risk, use the recovery position

treatments.description: Assess level of consciousness every 15 minutes using AVPU scale: Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive.
treatments.notes: Any deterioration in level of consciousness is a serious sign requiring urgent evacuation

treatments.description: For concussion: rest completely. No exercise, screens, or activities requiring concentration. Wake every 2 hours if sleeping to assess consciousness.

warningSigns: One pupil larger than the other (brain herniation — critical emergency)
Repeated vomiting (increased intracranial pressure)
Worsening headache
Clear fluid from ears or nose (CSF — skull fracture)
Deteriorating level of consciousness
Battle's sign: bruising behind the ears (delayed sign of skull fracture)
whenToEvacuate: Any loss of consciousness, confusion lasting more than 1 hour, worsening headache, vomiting, or unequal pupils — evacuate immediately.
preventionTips: Always wear a helmet for activities such as biking, climbing, and whitewater activities
Use trekking poles on uneven terrain to prevent falls
Never dive into unknown bodies of water

name: Sunburn
immediateActions: Get out of the sun
Apply cool compresses or cool (not cold) water
Stay hydrated

treatments.description: Cool the skin with cool water compresses. Do not use ice.
Apply aloe vera gel or a gentle moisturizing lotion. Avoid products with petroleum-based ingredients (trap heat) or benzocaine (can cause allergic reactions).
Take ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain and inflammation. Drink extra water.

warningSigns: Blistering over large areas (2nd degree burn)
Fever above 103°F
Signs of heat stroke accompanying sunburn
whenToEvacuate: Severe blistering sunburn covering large areas, or sunburn combined with heat stroke symptoms.
preventionTips: Apply SPF 30+ sunscreen 30 minutes before sun exposure, reapply every 2 hours
Wear UPF-rated clothing, wide-brimmed hat, and sunglasses
Seek shade between 10am and 4pm
Snow and water reflect UV and increase exposure significantly

name: Frostbite
symptoms: loss of sensation in affected area
skin turns red then purple or black in severe cases
immediateActions: Get out of the cold and wind immediately
Do NOT rub the affected area — this damages tissue
Do NOT rewarm if there is risk of refreezing — walking on a rewarmed foot causes more damage than the frostbite itself
Remove wet clothing and jewelry from the affected area
Protect the area from further cold and trauma

treatments.description: Assess depth: superficial (frostnip) — skin is soft underneath but numb and white. Deep frostbite — skin is hard and wooden throughout.
treatments.notes: Frostnip can be treated in the field. Deep frostbite requires evacuation for controlled rewarming.

treatments.description: For frostnip only: rewarm by placing affected area against warm skin — armpits for hands, warm abdomen for feet. Steady warmth for 20-30 minutes.
treatments.notes: Do not use fire, heating pads, or hot water — frostbitten skin cannot feel heat and will burn

treatments.description: If evacuation is more than 2 hours away and refreezing is not a risk, rewarm deep frostbite in water between 99-102°F (warm to the touch, not hot). Immerse for 15-30 minutes until tissue is soft and flushed.
treatments.notes: Rewarming is extremely painful — ibuprofen can help reduce pain and tissue damage if available

treatments.description: After rewarming, loosely wrap with sterile gauze. Place dry gauze between toes/fingers. Do not break blisters. Elevate if possible.

warningSigns: Hard, wooden-feeling tissue that does not soften with warmth (deep frostbite)
Black or mummified tissue (severe deep frostbite — tissue death)
Large blood-filled blisters (deep frostbite)
Frostbite combined with hypothermia — treat hypothermia first
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate all deep frostbite cases. Superficial frostnip that fully resolves with field rewarming may not require evacuation.
preventionTips: Dress in layers — wool or synthetic only, no cotton
Keep extremities dry — change wet gloves and socks immediately
Wiggle fingers and toes regularly in cold to maintain circulation
Buddy system — check each other's face and ears for white patches
Eat and stay hydrated — dehydration and low blood sugar accelerate frostbite

name: Burn
symptoms: white, brown, or black skin with little pain (3rd degree — nerve damage)
immediateActions: Cool the burn immediately with cool (not cold) running water for 10-20 minutes
Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or oil — these cause more damage
Remove jewelry and loose clothing near the burn before swelling begins
Do not break blisters
Cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing

treatments.description: Cool with cool running water for a full 10-20 minutes. This is the most important step and reduces tissue damage even if started several minutes after the burn.
treatments.notes: Do not use ice — it causes vasoconstriction and worsens tissue damage

treatments.description: Assess burn depth and area. 1st degree: red, painful, no blisters. 2nd degree: blisters, very painful. 3rd degree: leathery/charred skin, painless.
treatments.notes: Use the Rule of Nines to estimate burn area: each arm = 9%, each leg = 18%, chest = 18%, back = 18%, head = 9%

treatments.description: Cover with a clean non-stick dressing or cling wrap (does not stick to wound). Do not use fluffy cotton — fibers stick to the wound.
Give ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain. Keep the patient warm — large burns cause rapid heat and fluid loss.

warningSigns: 3rd degree burn — any size requires evacuation
Burns covering more than 10% of body surface (roughly one whole arm)
Burns to face, hands, feet, genitals, or over a joint
Burn caused by chemicals or electricity
Inhalation burn — singed nose hairs, hoarse voice, soot in mouth (airway emergency)
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate all 3rd degree burns, large 2nd degree burns, burns to critical areas, and any chemical or electrical burns. 1st degree and small 2nd degree burns can be managed in the field.
preventionTips: Never leave a fire unattended
Keep a safe distance from open flames — synthetic fabrics melt and stick to skin
Use a pot holder or gloves when handling hot cookware
Set up a fire perimeter to keep people a safe distance from open flames
Boiling water is a common burn source — always pour away from yourself

name: Shock
symptoms: loss of consciousness in severe cases
immediateActions: Treat the underlying cause if possible (control bleeding, treat burns, etc.)
Lay the patient flat and elevate the legs 8-12 inches unless head, neck, or spinal injury is suspected
Keep the patient warm — prevent heat loss with blankets
Do not give food or water by mouth
Monitor and reassess continuously

treatments.description: Identify and control the cause: stop severe bleeding with direct pressure or tourniquet, treat severe allergic reactions with epinephrine, cool heat stroke victims.
treatments.notes: Shock is a symptom, not a diagnosis — treating the cause is the priority

treatments.description: Position: lay flat on back, elevate legs 8-12 inches. If unconscious and breathing, use recovery position. If breathing is difficult, sit upright.
materials: pack or rolled clothing to elevate legs
treatments.notes: Do not elevate legs if you suspect head, spinal, hip, pelvis, or leg fracture

treatments.description: Prevent heat loss. Insulate from the ground and cover with blankets or a sleeping bag. A hypothermic shock victim deteriorates much faster.
Monitor vital signs every 5 minutes: pulse rate and quality, respiratory rate, skin color and temperature, level of consciousness.
treatments.notes: Record findings — trending is as important as individual readings for medical personnel

warningSigns: Pulse becomes absent or imperceptible — begin CPR
Breathing stops — begin CPR
Patient becomes unresponsive
Signs of internal bleeding: rigid or distended abdomen, bruising over abdomen or flanks
whenToEvacuate: All shock cases require immediate evacuation. Shock is life-threatening and cannot be definitively treated in the field.
preventionTips: Control bleeding rapidly and aggressively — blood loss is the most common shock cause in trauma
Carry a tourniquet and know how to use it
Keep patients warm after injuries — hypothermia worsens shock significantly
Know your group members' allergy history and whether they carry epinephrine

name: Tick Bite
symptoms: redness or mild swelling at bite site
bull's-eye rash (erythema migrans — sign of Lyme disease, appears 3-30 days after bite)
flu-like symptoms days after bite (fever, fatigue, headache, muscle aches)
joint pain weeks after untreated bite
immediateActions: Remove the tick as soon as possible — the longer it feeds, the higher the transmission risk
Use fine-tipped tweezers or a tick removal tool
Do NOT twist, crush, or use petroleum jelly, heat, or nail polish — these cause the tick to regurgitate into the wound

treatments.description: Grasp the tick with fine-tipped tweezers as close to the skin surface as possible. Pull upward with steady, even pressure — do not jerk or twist.
materials: fine-tipped tweezers or tick removal tool
treatments.notes: If mouthparts break off and remain in the skin, leave them and let the skin heal — do not dig for them

treatments.description: Clean the bite area thoroughly with soap and water, then apply antiseptic.
Save the tick in a sealed bag or container. Note the date and body location of the bite. This information helps medical personnel assess Lyme disease risk.
treatments.notes: Take a photo if a container is not available

treatments.description: Monitor the bite site and the patient for 30 days. Watch for a bull's-eye rash (expanding red ring) or flu-like symptoms.
treatments.notes: Lyme disease is treatable with antibiotics if caught early — report any symptoms to a doctor promptly

warningSigns: Bull's-eye rash (erythema migrans) — Lyme disease indicator, seek medical care
Fever, severe headache, or stiff neck within days of bite (possible tick-borne illness)
Paralysis or weakness beginning in the legs and spreading upward (tick paralysis — remove tick immediately)
Tick found in groin, behind ear, or in scalp — higher infection risk areas
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate if a bull's-eye rash develops, fever develops within 30 days of the bite, or if signs of tick paralysis appear.
preventionTips: Wear long pants tucked into socks and long sleeves in tick habitat
Apply DEET or permethrin-treated clothing
Do a full body tick check after every outing — focus on scalp, behind ears, armpits, groin, and behind knees
Check gear and pets before entering your shelter
Stay on trails and avoid brushing against tall grass and bushes

name: Choking
symptoms: sudden inability to speak or cry
high-pitched noise or no sound when breathing
immediateActions: Ask 'Are you choking?' — if they can speak or cough forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing
If they cannot speak, cry, or cough effectively, act immediately
For adults and children over 1 year: perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver)
For infants under 1 year: use back blows and chest thrusts — NOT abdominal thrusts
If the person becomes unconscious, begin CPR

treatments.description: Stand behind the person. Make a fist with one hand and place the thumb side against the abdomen, just above the navel and well below the breastbone. Grasp your fist with the other hand.
treatments.notes: For a pregnant person or someone too large to encircle, use chest thrusts against the lower breastbone instead

treatments.description: Give firm upward and inward abdominal thrusts. Repeat until the object is expelled or the person loses consciousness.
treatments.notes: Each thrust should be a distinct, forceful motion — not a squeeze

treatments.description: Self-choking: make a fist and thrust inward and upward against your own abdomen. Alternatively, lean over and drive your upper abdomen against a hard edge like a chair back or counter.

If the person loses consciousness, lower them carefully to the ground. Begin CPR. Each time you open the airway to give a breath, look in the mouth — if you can see the object, remove it with a finger sweep. Do not perform blind finger sweeps.
treatments.notes: CPR chest compressions can dislodge a foreign body airway obstruction

warningSigns: Cyanosis (blue lips or fingertips) — severe hypoxia
Loss of consciousness — begin CPR immediately
Abdominal thrusts are not dislodging the object after multiple attempts
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate after any choking episode that required abdominal thrusts — internal injury to the abdomen or airway is possible and requires medical evaluation.
preventionTips: Cut food into small pieces, especially for younger scouts
Do not eat while walking, running, or talking loudly
All trip leaders should be trained in CPR and the Heimlich maneuver
Keep small objects and hard candies away from young children

name: Altitude Sickness (AMS)
symptoms: shortness of breath at rest (severe AMS)
confusion or loss of coordination (HACE — brain swelling)
persistent dry cough or pink frothy sputum (HAPE — lung fluid)
immediateActions: Stop ascending immediately at first symptoms
Rest at current altitude — do not go higher
Descend if symptoms do not improve within 24 hours or worsen at any point
Descend immediately for any signs of HACE (confusion, loss of coordination) or HAPE (breathlessness at rest, gurgling cough)

treatments.description: Stop ascending. Rest at the same altitude. Mild AMS often resolves within 12-24 hours with acclimatization — do not push higher.
treatments.notes: The golden rule: never ascend with symptoms. 'If in doubt, go down.'

treatments.description: Stay hydrated. Drink 3-4 liters of water per day at altitude. Avoid alcohol and sedatives — both suppress breathing.
Ibuprofen (600mg every 8 hours) reduces AMS headache and has been shown to reduce AMS severity. Acetaminophen is an alternative for pain relief.
treatments.notes: Ibuprofen treats symptoms — it does not cure AMS or replace descent

treatments.description: If symptoms worsen or do not improve in 24 hours, descend a minimum of 300-500 meters (1,000-1,500 ft). Even a small descent can produce dramatic improvement.
treatments.notes: Descend immediately for any sign of HACE or HAPE — these are life-threatening emergencies

warningSigns: Loss of coordination or staggering gait (HACE — High Altitude Cerebral Edema): descend immediately
Confusion, severe headache unresponsive to medication, altered mental status (HACE)
Breathlessness at rest, persistent cough, gurgling sound in chest (HAPE — High Altitude Pulmonary Edema): descend immediately
Pink or frothy sputum (HAPE — advanced, critical)
Any AMS symptoms in a child — children deteriorate faster than adults
whenToEvacuate: Descend immediately for HACE or HAPE — both are life-threatening. Mild AMS that does not improve with 24 hours of rest at altitude also requires descent.
preventionTips: Ascend slowly: above 8,000 ft, limit sleeping altitude gain to 1,000 ft per day
Climb high, sleep low — daytime ascents are fine if you descend to sleep
Spend an extra acclimatization day for every 3,000 ft above 8,000 ft
Stay well hydrated throughout the trip
Avoid alcohol and sleeping pills the first nights at altitude
Know the symptoms — AMS can affect anyone regardless of fitness level

name: Bee / Wasp Sting
symptoms: immediate sharp pain at sting site
redness and swelling at sting site
multiple stings may cause nausea, fever, and dizziness
immediateActions: Move away from the area to avoid further stings
Remove the stinger if present — scrape out with a fingernail or card edge, do not squeeze it
Watch closely for signs of allergic reaction for at least 30 minutes
Have epinephrine ready if the person has a known bee allergy

treatments.description: Remove the stinger by scraping with a fingernail, credit card edge, or knife blade. Do not use tweezers — squeezing injects more venom.
treatments.notes: Only honeybees leave a stinger — wasps, hornets, and yellow jackets do not

treatments.description: Wash the sting site with soap and water. Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for 20 minutes to reduce pain and swelling.
materials: cold pack or ice in cloth

treatments.description: Apply hydrocortisone cream or calamine lotion if available. Oral antihistamine (Benadryl) reduces itching and mild localized swelling.
For multiple stings (20+ in adults, fewer in children): treat as potentially serious. Lay the patient down, monitor closely for systemic reaction, and prepare to evacuate.
treatments.notes: Mass envenomation from multiple stings can cause toxic reaction even without allergy

warningSigns: Hives, swelling of face or throat, difficulty breathing — anaphylaxis, use EpiPen immediately
Dizziness, rapid pulse, or drop in blood pressure
Swelling that spreads rapidly beyond the sting site
Sting inside the mouth or throat — airway swelling risk even without allergy
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate immediately for any systemic allergic reaction. Single uncomplicated stings do not require evacuation.
preventionTips: Avoid wearing bright floral patterns and sweet-scented perfumes or lotions
Keep food and drinks covered — yellow jackets are attracted to sugary drinks
Stay calm and move slowly away from a bee — swatting triggers attack
Check shoes and clothing before putting them on outdoors
Know your group members' allergy history before every trip

name: Spider Bite
symptoms: immediate or delayed pain at bite site
black widow: severe muscle cramps and pain spreading from bite (30-60 min after bite), sweating, nausea, rigid abdomen
brown recluse: expanding bulls-eye lesion, blister that becomes a necrotic ulcer over days
headache, nausea, fever in moderate to severe envenomation
immediateActions: Stay calm and limit movement — this slows venom spread
Try to identify or photograph the spider without getting bitten again
Clean the wound with soap and water
Apply a cold pack to reduce pain and swelling

treatments.description: Wash the bite area thoroughly with soap and water. Apply antiseptic.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to the bite site for 20 minutes at a time to reduce pain and slow venom absorption.
materials: cold pack or ice in cloth

treatments.description: Immobilize and elevate the affected limb. Mark the edge of any redness or swelling with a pen and record the time to track spread.

For black widow bites with muscle cramps: ibuprofen and muscle relaxants if available. Antivenom exists — evacuation is the priority. For brown recluse: wound will develop slowly; medical wound care and possible antibiotic treatment required.
treatments.notes: Most spider bites in the US are not from dangerous species. If uncertain, treat as moderate and monitor closely.

warningSigns: Severe spreading muscle cramps, rigid abdomen, sweating (black widow envenomation)
Expanding necrotic wound — skin turning dark and dying (brown recluse)
Difficulty breathing or swallowing
Systemic symptoms: high fever, seizures, altered consciousness
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate for confirmed or suspected black widow or brown recluse bites, significant systemic symptoms, or expanding necrotic wound.
preventionTips: Shake out shoes, clothing, and sleeping bags before use
Wear gloves when moving logs, rocks, or debris
Check inside dark dry spaces — black widows favor wood piles and outhouses
Use a headlamp when moving around camp at night
Keep the tent zipped closed at all times

name: Puncture Wound
immediateActions: Do NOT remove deeply embedded objects — they may be controlling bleeding
Control surface bleeding with gentle pressure around (not on) an embedded object
Do NOT close or seal a puncture wound — it needs to drain
Clean the wound thoroughly with irrigation

treatments.description: If an object is embedded: stabilize it in place with bulky padding on either side and bandage around it. Do not remove it — it may be preventing major bleeding.
treatments.notes: Only remove embedded objects if they are very shallow, small, and not near the eye, chest, or abdomen

treatments.description: If no object is embedded: irrigate forcefully with clean water using a syringe or squeeze bottle. The narrow channel is impossible to clean without pressure irrigation.
treatments.notes: A plastic bag with a pinhole makes an effective improvised irrigation syringe

treatments.description: Do NOT close the wound. Leave it open to drain. Apply a loose non-occlusive dressing.
treatments.notes: Closing a puncture wound traps bacteria and dramatically increases infection risk

treatments.description: Monitor daily for infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaking from the wound. Change dressing daily.
treatments.notes: Puncture wounds have high infection risk due to depth and difficulty cleaning — tetanus risk is also elevated

warningSigns: Red streaking spreading from wound (cellulitis or blood poisoning — urgent)
Pus, increasing warmth, and swelling after 24-48 hours
Wound in chest or abdomen — risk of internal organ injury
Numbness or inability to move below the wound (nerve or tendon involvement)
Puncture from an animal or human bite — high infection risk requiring antibiotics
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate for deeply embedded objects, wounds to the chest or abdomen, animal or human bites, signs of infection, or if tetanus vaccination is not current.
preventionTips: Always wear footwear in camp — stepping on sticks and stakes is a common cause
Carry and use a walking stick to probe terrain ahead
Handle knives and sharp tools with care — cut away from the body
Keep camp tidy — tent stakes and wire are puncture hazards

name: Seizure
symptoms: loss of bladder or bowel control
brief periods of confusion or staring (absence seizure)
immediateActions: Do NOT restrain the person or put anything in their mouth
Clear the area of hard and sharp objects that could cause injury
Cushion the head with something soft
Time the seizure — duration matters for medical assessment
After convulsions stop, place in recovery position to prevent choking

treatments.description: Protect from injury: move hard objects away, cushion the head, do not restrict movement. Stay with the person throughout.
materials: folded clothing or pack for head cushion
treatments.notes: Never put your fingers or any object in the mouth — this risks injury to you and broken teeth for them. The tongue cannot be swallowed.

treatments.description: Time the seizure from start to finish. Note what happened: which body parts were involved, loss of consciousness, eye movement direction.
treatments.notes: This information is critical for medical personnel to determine seizure type and cause

treatments.description: After convulsions stop, place in the recovery position (on their side). Keep the airway open. The person will be confused and exhausted (postictal state) — reassure them calmly.
treatments.notes: Postictal confusion can last minutes to hours — this is normal and does not mean the seizure is continuing

treatments.description: If the person is a known epileptic, ask if they have rescue medication (such as diazepam). Follow their established care plan. If seizure was unprovoked and first-time, treat as unknown cause.

warningSigns: Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus — life-threatening)
Seizure occurs in water — drowning risk is immediate
Person does not regain consciousness after convulsions stop
Second seizure follows without recovery in between
Seizure with head injury, fever, or after a recent head trauma
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate for any first-time seizure, seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, seizure in water, or failure to regain consciousness. Known epileptics who recover fully per their normal pattern may not require evacuation.
preventionTips: Know if any group members have a seizure disorder before the trip
Ensure known epileptics have and carry their medication
Avoid seizure triggers: missed medication, sleep deprivation, alcohol, and extreme heat
Buddy system near water for anyone with a seizure history

name: Cardiac Arrest / CPR
symptoms: no normal breathing (absent or only gasping)
no pulse (may be checked at carotid artery in the neck)
immediateActions: Call for help loudly — assign someone to call 911 or activate emergency locator
Check for responsiveness: tap shoulders, shout
Check for normal breathing for no more than 10 seconds
If unresponsive and not breathing normally, begin CPR immediately
Send someone to get an AED if available — do not stop CPR to find one

treatments.description: Position the person on their back on a firm, flat surface. Kneel beside their chest.

Chest compressions: place heel of hand on center of chest (lower half of breastbone). Place second hand on top, interlace fingers. Compress hard and fast — at least 2 inches deep, at least 100-120 compressions per minute. Allow full chest recoil between each compression.
treatments.notes: Push hard — effective compressions require significant force. Rib fractures are a known CPR complication but do not stop compressions.

treatments.description: Rescue breaths (if trained and willing): after 30 compressions, tilt head back, lift chin, pinch nose, give 2 breaths over 1 second each — enough to see the chest rise. Return to compressions immediately. Ratio: 30 compressions : 2 breaths.
treatments.notes: Hands-only CPR (compressions without breaths) is acceptable and effective, especially for untrained bystanders or when a face shield is unavailable

treatments.description: If an AED is available: power it on and follow voice prompts exactly. Apply pads, clear the patient before each shock, and resume CPR immediately after each shock. Do not interrupt CPR while the AED is analyzing.
treatments.notes: The AED will tell you everything to do — trust the device. Every minute without defibrillation reduces survival by 10%.

treatments.description: Continue CPR until: the person shows signs of life (breathing, movement), an AED advises no shock and person is breathing, a trained rescuer takes over, or you are physically unable to continue.
treatments.notes: Switch compressors every 2 minutes if possible — fatigue causes compression quality to drop rapidly

warningSigns: Agonal breathing (slow, gasping, irregular breaths) — this is not normal breathing; begin CPR
Absence of carotid pulse for more than 10 seconds
whenToEvacuate: Immediate evacuation is required. Cardiac arrest has near-zero wilderness survival without definitive care. Continue CPR until emergency services arrive or take over.
preventionTips: All trip leaders should be current in CPR and first aid certification
Know the cardiac history of your group members before strenuous trips
Carry an AED on large group trips if available
Recognize warning signs before arrest: chest pain, left arm or jaw pain, sudden severe shortness of breath, extreme fatigue during mild exertion

name: Shoulder Dislocation
symptoms: visibly deformed shoulder — squared off or with a bump below the normal shoulder contour
arm held close to the body and supported by the other arm
numbness or tingling down the arm
immediateActions: Do not attempt to relocate the shoulder without training — improper reduction can fracture the humerus or damage nerves and blood vessels
Immobilize the arm in the position of comfort using a sling
Apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce pain and swelling
Check and record circulation, sensation, and movement in the hand

treatments.description: Check CSM (Circulation, Sensation, Movement) in the hand: capillary refill, ability to feel light touch, ability to wiggle fingers. Document findings and recheck every 15 minutes.
treatments.notes: Loss of pulse or sensation in the hand is a vascular or nerve emergency

treatments.description: Immobilize with a sling and swathe: use a triangular bandage or improvised sling to support the arm across the chest, then bind the arm to the chest with a second wrap.
Apply ice or cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce pain and swelling. Give ibuprofen for pain and inflammation.
materials: cold pack or ice in cloth

treatments.description: Self-reduction (only if evacuation will be prolonged and the person is trained): the FARES method — lay patient on back, extend arm at 90° with elbow straight, apply gentle traction while slowly rotating externally and elevating. Stop if significant resistance is felt.
treatments.notes: Reduction is easier within the first 30 minutes before muscle spasm sets in. After 30 minutes, pain and spasm make field reduction much harder.

warningSigns: Loss of radial pulse in the wrist (axillary artery injury)
Complete numbness or paralysis of the arm (axillary nerve injury)
Associated fracture — grinding sensation or visible bone (do not attempt reduction)
whenToEvacuate: Evacuate all dislocations for imaging and professional reduction. If evacuation is more than 6-8 hours away, field reduction by a trained individual is appropriate.
preventionTips: Use trekking poles to reduce fall impact on the upper body
Avoid catching yourself on an outstretched arm during a fall — tuck and roll instead
Strengthen rotator cuff muscles before high-demand trips
Prior dislocation is the biggest risk factor — those with history should discuss preventive bracing with a doctor

name: Near Drowning (Submersion)
symptoms: water expelled from mouth or nose
hypothermia signs if water was cold
immediateActions: Remove the person from the water safely — do not become a victim yourself
Call for emergency help immediately
If unresponsive and not breathing, begin CPR immediately — do not wait
All submersion victims require medical evaluation even if they appear to have recovered

treatments.description: Ensure scene safety before entering water. Use a rope, clothing, or floating object to reach the victim without entering if possible — a panicking victim can pull a rescuer under.
treatments.notes: Reach, throw, row, then go — attempt passive rescue before entering the water

treatments.description: If unresponsive and not breathing: begin CPR immediately. For drowning, give 5 rescue breaths first before starting compressions — hypoxia is the primary problem.
treatments.notes: Do not waste time trying to drain water from the lungs — it is ineffective. Begin CPR immediately.

treatments.description: If breathing: place in recovery position on their side to allow water and vomit to drain. Monitor breathing continuously — respiratory status can deteriorate rapidly in the hours after submersion.
treatments.notes: Secondary drowning (delayed pulmonary edema) can occur 1-24 hours after a submersion event — all victims need hospital evaluation

treatments.description: Treat for hypothermia if the water was cold — remove wet clothing, insulate, and provide warmth. Cold water submersion victims may survive longer without oxygen due to the mammalian dive reflex.
treatments.notes: Hypothermic cardiac arrest from cold water: 'not dead until warm and dead' — continue CPR until rewarmed

warningSigns: Any period of unconsciousness during or after submersion
Persistent coughing, shortness of breath, or gurgling chest sounds after recovery (secondary drowning)
Altered mental status after apparent recovery
Suspected spinal injury — diving or fall into water
whenToEvacuate: All submersion victims require hospital evaluation regardless of apparent recovery. Secondary drowning can be fatal hours after the event.
preventionTips: Always wear a life jacket during water activities — including canoe, kayak, and raft trips
Never swim alone — always use the buddy system
Know your swimming ability and respect currents and cold water
Cold water shock can cause involuntary gasping and muscle failure within 1 minute — even strong swimmers can drown
All leaders should be water rescue trained for trips near water
